coredata.dc:title: Study on a novel Si3N4-based composite with the incorporation of N-GQDs produced from nano-lignin
coredata.dc:creator: Chen, Wei
Xu, Enguang
Liu, Xingyu
Zhao, Ziqiang
Li, Zehao
Zhu, Ping
Lou, Rui
Zhang, Zhiyun
Li, Huaqiang
coredata.dc:description: In order to enhance the mechanical properties of ceramic materials and realize the high value utilization of lignin, the nano-lignin precursor was added into the silicon nitride matrix as the second phase to obtain a novel ceramic composite material with excellent mechanical performance in this study. During the preparation, the nano-lignin was transformed into nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots(N-GQDs) in the ceramic matrix by the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process. Aiming to reveal the pyrolysis conversion process of lignin, the segmented products at the different temperature gradient were prepared, and their phase composition and microstructure were deeply analyzed by various test methods. In addition, the continuous pyrolysis process of nano-lignin was further studied by simultaneous thermal analyzer. The results showed that, nano-lignin was transformed into solid products (amorphous carbon) in the temperature range of room temperature to 800 °C and produced a large number of volatiles. Subsequently, in the temperature range of 800∼1400 °C, part of amorphous carbon began to transform into N-GQDs, and part of α-Si3N4 also began to transform into β-Si3N4. When the temperature rose to the temperature of 1700 °C, all amorphous carbon was transformed into N-GQDs through carbon structure rearrangement and nitrogen doping, and meanwhile the most of α-Si3N4 was transformed into β-Si3N4. At the final stage, β-Si3N4 columnar grains were covered by an overlayer composed of N-GQDs, and a novel ceramic composite with a special “core-shell” microstructure was obtained and exhibited excellent mechanical performance. This study presents a promising approach for the preparation of high-performance ceramic materials, and can also intrigue great interests in lignin graphitization and lignin-derived carbon materials.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Si3N4
Nano-lignin
N-GQDs
Pyrolysis

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: The authors are thankful for the funding provided by the Open Fund of the National Joint Engineering Research Center for abrasion control and molding of metal materials (grant number No. HKDNM201902 ), Chinese Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number No. 2019M653630 ), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (grant number No. 2018JM5056 ) and Xi'an Science and Technology Program (grant number No. 23ZDCYJSGG0047-2022 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The time-temperature curve of the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process.
Microstructure morphology of segmented products: a) starting powder, b) 800 °C-0min, c) 800 °C-30min, d) 1400 °C-0min, e) 1700 °C-0min, f) 1700 °C-60min.

Microstructure of corrosion surface and cross section of SN-2L and related selective electron diffraction patterns ((a) is FESEM image of the etched surface of the SN-2L sample, (b) is the red arrow section of Fig.3(a), (c）is the enlarged picture of the red frame area in the middle and lower sides of Fig.3(b)) [14].
XRD patterns of segmented products of Si3N4-based composite.
Raman spectra of segmented products for Si3N4-based composite at the different gradient.
FTIR spectra of the starting powder, 800 °C-0min, 800 °C-30min ((a) is the total spectrum, (b) is the local magnification).
FTIR spectra of 1400 °C-0min, 1700 °C-0min, 1700 °C-60min ((a) is the total spectrum, (b) is the left local magnification view, and (c) is the right local magnification view).
TG-DSC curve of nano-lignin-silicon nitride matrix composite.
Schematical illustration of coupling process of pyrolytic-hot-pressing sintering for the Si3N4-based composite.
Chemical composition of the starting powders.
Physical and mechanical properties of the segmented products.
Physical and mechanical properties of Si3N4 and Si3N4-based composites doped with nano-lignin.
Relative content of β-Si3N4 in Si3N4-based composite at different gradients.
Characteristic peak intensity, ID/IG and I2D/IG ratios of Raman spectra for the segmented products.

row.entry: The full width at half maximum of D peak（cm-1）

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis data of FT-IR characteristic absorption peaks for SN0, SN1, SN2.

row.entry: C–H in-plane stretching vibration of aromatic nucleus
Out-of-plane deformation vibration of benzene ring C–H

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Analysis of FT-IR characteristic absorption peaks of SN3, SN4, SN5.

C–H in-plane stretching vibration of aromatic nucleus
Out-of-plane deformation vibration of benzene ring C–H

head.ce:title: Study on a novel Si3N4-based composite with the incorporation of N-GQDs produced from nano-lignin

Xi'an University of Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi'an Jiaotong University, Xi'an, 710049, Shaanxi, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: State Key Laboratory of Electrical Insulation and Power Equipment, Xi’an Jiaotong University, Xi’an, 710049, Shaanxi, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In order to enhance the mechanical properties of ceramic materials and realize the high value utilization of lignin, the nano-lignin precursor was added into the silicon nitride matrix as the second phase to obtain a novel ceramic composite material with excellent mechanical performance in this study. During the preparation, the nano-lignin was transformed into nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots(N-GQDs) in the ceramic matrix by the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process. Aiming to reveal the pyrolysis conversion process of lignin, the segmented products at the different temperature gradient were prepared, and their phase composition and microstructure were deeply analyzed by various test methods. In addition, the continuous pyrolysis process of nano-lignin was further studied by simultaneous thermal analyzer. The results showed that, nano-lignin was transformed into solid products (amorphous carbon) in the temperature range of room temperature to 800 °C and produced a large number of volatiles. Subsequently, in the temperature range of 800∼1400 °C, part of amorphous carbon began to transform into N-GQDs, and part of α-Si3N4 also began to transform into β-Si3N4. When the temperature rose to the temperature of 1700 °C, all amorphous carbon was transformed into N-GQDs through carbon structure rearrangement and nitrogen doping, and meanwhile the most of α-Si3N4 was transformed into β-Si3N4. At the final stage, β-Si3N4 columnar grains were covered by an overlayer composed of N-GQDs, and a novel ceramic composite with a special “core-shell” microstructure was obtained and exhibited excellent mechanical performance. This study presents a promising approach for the preparation of high-performance ceramic materials, and can also intrigue great interests in lignin graphitization and lignin-derived carbon materials.

ce:section.ce:para: In 1857, lignin was proposed by Schulz and derived from the Latin 'lignum', meaning wood [1]. In nature, lignin is the second largest natural biomass resource after cellulose, and it is also the most abundant aromatic renewable resource [2,3]. Meanwhile, in the pulp and paper industry, lignin has long been regarded as a waste by-product, and can provide heat and energy for the pulping process through combustion treatment [4,5]. In recent years, with the further understanding of lignin, the transformation of low-cost lignin into high-value carbon nanomaterials opens up a new high-value utilization way of lignin.

Among the transformation methods, it is very valuable that lignin can be used as precursor to obtain the relevant carbon nanomaterials by using pyrolysis way. The pyrolysis process of lignin generally contains two aspects of reactions: primary reaction and secondary reaction. In primary reaction, the solid products (such as carbon nanomaterials) are directly formed from decomposition of the lignin; in the secondary reaction, gaseous products are volatiles produced during the pyrolysis process [6]. During the early study of lignin pyrolysis [7,8] found that, at the pyrosis temperature of 800 °C, coke was the solid pyrolysis products of lignin precursor, while the carbon nanomaterials have been not still gained as the pyrolysis products of lignin. At present, more scholars devoted to the research for the lignin pyrolysis, and found that lignin could be pyrolyzed into carbon nanomaterials (e.g., graphene materials) at higher temperatures [3,9].

Until now, lignin was usually added to the functional material matrix as a second phase or a second phase precursor for graphene to improve electrical, optical and electrochemical properties [10–13]. For example, Yu [10] et al. prepared bulk woodceramic electrode materials by Ni(NO3)2 catalysis and K2CO3 activation using waste poplar wood self-assembled from pulping black liquor lignin as raw material. During the sintering process, lignin was converted into carbon nanosheet and multilayer graphene on the surface of the pore structure. When the current density was 0.25 A g−1, the electrode material could reach a specific capacitance of 150.8 F g−1.

However, lignin is rarely added into structural material matrix as the second phase aiming to enhance the mechanical or tribological performance. Therefore, our research group [14,15] specially added nano-lignin as the precursor into silicon nitride starting powder and then prepared silicon nitride matrix composites by pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process. In the coupling process, nano-lignin was transformed into N-GQDs, and a novel ceramic composite with core-shell structure was prepared with excellent mechanical properties. It is obvious that, the pyrosis products-GQDs of nano-lignin is very beneficial to the microstructure and mechanical properties of silicon nitride ceramic, but it is not clear how nano-lignin can be transformed into graphene quantum dots in a silicon nitride matrix during the coupling process.

In view to the researches above, this paper studied deeply the pyrolysis transformation mechanism of nano-lignin in ceramic matrix by pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process. The pyrolysis transformation mechanism was obviously revealed though segmented pyrolysis product detection method under the planned temperature gradients and thermogravimetric analysis. This study laid a foundation for exploring the effects of lignin incorporation on the properties of silicon nitride composites, and provided a new way for the high value utilization of lignin in the field of structural materials.

In this study, nano-lignin extracted from poplar by deep eutectic solvent [16] was selected as the precursor and added into the ceramic starting powders. The commercial silicon nitride powder (purity >99.9 %, α phase mass fraction >94 %, HeFei Aijia New Material Science & Technology, Ltd, China) was used as the ceramic powder in the present study. Y2O3 (purity >99.9 %, average particle size of 0.37 μm) and Al2O3 (purity >99.9 %, average particle size of 1.17 μm) were used as sintering additives. Based on our previous studies [14,15], raw materials were mixed according to the ratio in Table 1 to obtain the starting powders.

The preparation of ceramic composites consisted of two steps: initial powder preparation and composite sample preparation. In the first step, the starting powders were mixed with ethanol and milled for 3 h at a speed of 150 r/min by using a BM40 planetary ball mill. Then, the mixed powders were dried at 30 °C for 12 h in an electric blast drying oven and sieved through a 150 mesh stainless steel mesh. In the second step, the mixed powders were placed in a graphite mold in a hot pressing sintering furnace (HIGH MULTI 5000, Japan), and the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process was carried out at a pressure of 30 MPa under a nitrogen environment. Under such experimental conditions, the mixed starting powders were firstly heated to 800 °C held for 30 min, and then heated to 1700 °C for 60 min, as shown in Fig. 1 . After the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process, the samples were cooled to room temperature in the furnace.

According to the relevant references [10,11,17–19], the pyrolysis transformation of lignin to amorphous carbon took place in a broad range from 200 to 900 °C, and further the transformation of amorphous carbon to graphene materials took place in the range from 1200 to 1400 °C. In view to the available research, five process gradients were selected in this study, and the segmented products were deeply analyzed in order to reveal the pyrolysis transformation mechanism of nano-lignin in the Si3N4 ceramic matrix, as shown in Fig. 1. The selected five process gradients were described as follows: (1) the starting powders was just heated to 800 °C; (2) The starting powders had been heated to 800 °C for 30 min; (3) The powders had been continuously heated to 1400 °C; (4) The sample was just heated to 1700 °C; (5) The sample had been heated to 1700 °C for 60 min. At each gradient, the experiment was repeated three times to confirm the consistency of the samples, and the typical samples were selected as the objects for further test and analysis. In order to facilitate the subsequent expression of the paper, the segmented product was numbered, as shown in Table 2 .

The microstructures of the segmented products were observed by high-resolution scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Verios 460, USA). Meanwhile, the phase compositions of the segmented products were determined by X-ray diffraction (XRD, D/Max2200/PC, Japan). In addition, the chemical structures, phases and morphologies, crystallinity of the segmented products were comprehensively analyzed by micro-Raman imaging spectrometer (Thermo Fisher SXRxi, USA).

According to GB/T 25995-2010, the volume density and apparent porosity of the segmented products were determined by Archimedes method in distilled water. Meanwhile, the hardness of the segmented product was measured by Vickers indentation method under the condition of 10 N load with a dwell time of 15 s according to GB/T 16534-2009.The segmented product was also processed into a cube of 3 mm × 4 mm × 35 mm, and the mirror was polished with a diamond slurry in order to the three-point bending strength test was performed at a speed of 0.5 mm/min and a span of 30 mm at room temperature to determine the bending strength. In addition, the fracture toughness of the segmented products was examined by three-point loading and single-edge notched beam technique with a span of 16 mm and a speed of 0.05 mm/min. All of the above tests were repeated 5 times.

In order to further reveal the essential causes of pyrolysis transformation in the coupled process of pyrolytic-hot pressing sintering, the molecular structure and chemical composition of the pyrolysis products were analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR, vertex70, Germany). In this study, potassium bromide tablet method was used to measure Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. The segmented products were added into an appropriate amount of potassium bromide, mixed under dry conditions, ground evenly, and then tableted, and placed on the Fourier transform infrared spectrometer for Fourier infrared analysis and detection.

In order to further elucidate the mechanism of pyrolysis and transformation of nano-lignin, the relationship between mass, heat flow and time during continuous pyrolysis was studied by using simultaneous thermal analyzer (TG, STA8000-Spectrum 3-Clarus690 SQ8C, USA). That is, the mixed starting powders were continuously heated from room temperature to 1200 °C at a heating rate of 10 °C/min by using nitrogen as a protective gas.

Table 3 shows the physical and mechanical properties of the segmented products for the ceramic composite. In the temperature range of room temperature to 800 °C, the segmented products are composed of powder, but the relevant physical and mechanical properties can only be detected on the bulk material. In the temperature range of 1400–1700 °C, the bending strength and fracture toughness increased gradually with the preparation evolution. When temperature increased to 1700 °C for 1 h, the bending strength and fracture toughness of segmented products reached 998.98 ± 18.66 MPa and 9.09 ± 0.22 MPa m1/2. Meanwhile, the bulk density and apparent porosity present the similar trendy. With the preparation evolution, the bulk density increased to 3.25 ± 0.03 g cm−3, and the apparent porosity decreased to 0.25 ± 0.02 %.

Table 4 shows the physical and mechanical properties of Si3N4 [14] and SN5. It can be seen from Table 4 that SN5 exhibits better comprehensive mechanical properties than Si3N4. The hardness, bending strength and fracture toughness of SN5 can reach 18.22 ± 0.69 GPa, 988.98 ± 18.66 MPa and 9.09 ± 0.22 MPa m1/2, respectively.

Fig. 2 shows the corresponding micro-morphologies of the segmented products for the composite. When the starting powders are not sintered, the nano-lignin is uniformly dispersed in the silicon nitride powders (Fig. 2(a)). For SN1 and SN2 at 800 °C, the powder is agglomerated (Fig. 2 (b ∼ c)). For SN3 at 1400 °C, the silicon nitride grains are partially covered by a white attachment layer (Fig. 2(d)), meaning the initial formation of N-GQDs. For SN4 and SN5 at 1700 °C, β-Si3N4 columnar grains are covered by one overlayer consisting of N-GQDs, forming a core-shell structure (indicated by the arrows in Fig. 2(e ∼ f)). The overlayer consisting of N-GQDs around silicon nitride columnar grains should be the cause of the incomplete phase transition of silicon nitride.

In order to further analyze the composition of the shell material on the surface of the silicon nitride grains, FIB focused ion beam thinning was performed on the cross section of the SN-2L grains in Fig. 3 (a), and the field emission scanning electron microscope was used to observe it. As shown in Fig. 3(b)–a large number of black dots with good crystallinity are coated around the grains. It can be seen from the selected area electron diffraction pattern at the upper right of (b) that the matrix material is hexagonal silicon nitride with (100), (101), (102) and (200), (210) crystal planes. The selected area electron diffraction pattern at the lower right is the diffraction spots of graphene quantum dots: (111), (311) and (400). Therefore, it can be confirmed that the shell structure is polycrystalline carbon with graphene quantum dots as the main component. Combined with Fig. 3(c), it can be seen that the interlayer spacing is about 0.23 nm, and the cross-sectional area is 8.99 nm2. In addition, the irregular diffraction spots of silicon nitride are caused by the coverage of silicon nitride grains by graphene quantum dots [14].

Fig. 4 shows the XRD results of segmented products. From the figure, the starting powder (SN0 specimen) is mainly composed of α-Si3N4, and the carbon materials are not detected. At the temperature gradient of 800 °C, the SN1 and SN2 specimens are composed of α-Si3N4 and a small amount of liquid phase Y2Si3O3N4 [20–22]. At the temperature gradient of 1400 °C, the SN3 specimen is composed of β-Si3N4 and Si5AlON7. At the temperature gradient of 1700 °C, SN4 and SN5 specimens are mainly composed of β-Si3N4. Meanwhile, for the segmented products of higher temperature than 800 °C (SN3, SN4 and SN5 specimens), a characteristic peak at 2θ = 27° can be always detected, and the peak is corresponding to (002) crystal plane (ICDD: 00-001-0640) of carbon related materials [23].

Table 5 shows the contents of beta phase in the segmented product for Si3N4-based composite, according to the following equation [24,25].
(1)

where, I
α(102) and I
α(210) represent the intensities of α-phase at planes (102) and (210)(ICDD: 04-005-5074), and I
β(101) and I
β(210) represent the intensities of β-phase at planes (101) and (210) (ICDD: 04-005-4900).

From Tables 5 and it can be seen that, as the preparation process progresses, the α-Si3N4 is gradually transformed into the beta phase, but even at 1700 °C, the alpha phase is not fully transformed into the beta phase. Combined with Fig. 4 and Tables 5 and it can be concluded that, with the addition of nano-lignin precursor, the phase transition from α-Si3N4 to β-Si3N4 should be inhibited during the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process, and the nano-lignin undergo carbonization during the preparation process.

The segmented products are also characterized by Raman spectroscopy in this study. As shown in Fig. 5 , only the characteristic peaks of α-Si3N4 are also detected for SN0 specimen. At the temperature gradient of 800 °C, D peak (characterizing the degree of disorder of carbon materials) and the G peak (characterizing the structural integrity of graphite carbon) [26] can be detected for SN1 and SN2 specimens. This represents that the original carbon structure of nano-lignin changes to graphite-like structure at the temperature of 800 °C. At the temperature gradient of 1400 °C, the intensity of D peak and G peak decreases, and 2D peak (about 2700 cm−1 indicating the stacking mode of carbon atoms and the layer structure) is detected for SN3 specimen. It is well known that, the 2D peak is independent of defects and can be used as a characteristic peak to identify graphene and graphite [27]. At this stage, the appearance of 2D peak proves that, nano-lignin in the ceramic matrix has been transformed into N-GQDs at 1400 °C. At the temperature gradient of 1700 °C, the intensity of the 2D peak further increases, indicating the N-GQDs with higher crystalline.

The intensity ratio of D peak to G peak (ID/IG) can directly reflect the degree of graphitization (R), the disorder and structural integrity of carbon materials [11,23,28,29]. Table 6 shows that, the value of ID/IG increases from 0.73 for SN1 at 800 °C to 1.33 at SN3 at 1400 °C, illustrating that the decrease of degree for graphitization of nano-lignin derivatives. In the temperature range from 800 °C to 1400 °C, the carbon structure of nano-lignin monomer would change from benzene ring structure to carbon six-membered ring structure, and some gaseous products (such as CH4, CO, CO2, H2O and H2) are formed [29]. The gaseous products should have a negative effect on the arrangement of carbon six-membered rings, which reduces the degree of graphitization and increase the degree of disorder [29]. It can be inferred that, the solid product of nano lignin mainly exists in the form of amorphous carbon at 800 °C, and the solid product of nano lignin is in the form of N-GQDs and amorphous carbon at 1400 °C.

In the temperature range from 1400 °C to 1700 °C, the ID/IG value decreases from 1.33 for SN3 at 1400 °C to 0.51 for SN5 at 1700 °C, indicating that the degree of graphitization of nano lignin derived solid products becomes higher, and the degree of disorder of carbon material structure becomes lower. Meanwhile, the 2D peak is more obvious and the intensity is further enhanced, which indicates that, the solid derivatives of nano lignin mainly exist in the form of N-GQDs (which are stacked in multiple layers) when the temperature increases to 1700 °C.

In addition, the full width at half maximum of the D peak of the graphite-like material with good crystallinity is generally between 40 cm−1 and 60 cm−1 [29,30]. In this study, in the temperature range from 1400 °C to 1700 °C, the full width at half maximum of the D peak of the sample is between 50 cm−1 and 60 cm−1. This shows that, at 1400 °C or higher, the carbon structure of the sample is highly similar to that of the crystalloid material, both of which have smaller crystal defects and higher crystallinity.

In this study, the segmented products were also analyzed by Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy. Fig. 6 shows the FTIR spectrum of SN0, SN1 and SN2, and Table 7 gives the corresponding attribution of characteristic absorption. The strong absorption peak at 1100∼840 cm−1 represents the tensile vibration of silicon nitride. The absorbance of SN1 and SN2 decreases, and no new characteristic peaks appear.

Fig. 7 shows the FTIR spectrum of SN3, SN4 and SN5, and Table 8 gives the corresponding attribution of characteristic absorption. It can be seen that, the characteristic absorption peaks of C–N tensile vibration appear at 1400∼1200 cm−1 and the characteristic absorption peaks of aromatic skeleton vibration appear at 1660∼1400 cm−1 for SN3 samples. The characteristic absorption peak of C–N tensile vibration appears at 1400 °C, indicating that C–N bond is formed at the range of 800–1400 °C and nitrogen doping occurs. For SN4 and SN5 samples, the absorbance of the characteristic absorption peak of the aromatic core skeleton vibration is enhanced, and the characteristic peak associated with the benzene ring no longer appears.

In this study, with the preparation process progresses, the absorption peak intensity related to the benzene ring structure for the segmented products decreases gradually, indicating that the benzene ring structure changes into an aromatic structure [29]. At the same time, the sample has a characteristic absorption peak of aromatic nuclear skeleton vibration at 1400–1700 °C, further indicating that the aromatic nuclear skeleton structure of graphene quantum dots has been formed [29,30].

According to the comprehensive observation of Figs. 6 and 7, combined with the above studies, it can be inferred that the reduction of characteristic peak intensity related to silicon nitride during the preparation process should be attributed to the overlayer of nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots on the surface of silicon nitride.

Based on the above analysis, it can be confirmed that, nano-lignin is carbonized to form a solid product of amorphous carbon with a high degree of disorder at the temperature of 800 °C; when the sample is further heated to 1400 °C, part of α-Si3N4 transforms to β-Si3N4, and part of amorphous carbon continues to transform into the graphene phase; when the sample is finally heated to 1700 °C for 1h, the phase transition of silicon nitride is basically over, and the amorphous carbon transformed from nano-lignin is completely transformed into multi-layer stacked N-GQDs. Finally, β-Si3N4 are covered by an overlayer composed of multi-layer stacked N-GQDs.

In order to further confirm the continuous transformation process of the sample, the thermogravimetric analysis of the starting powder was performed to reveal its thermogravimetric behavior. In the thermogravimetric analysis, the pyrolytic-hot pressing sintering process were simulated as far as possible, and TG and DSC curves are shown in Fig. 8 .

It can be seen from Fig. 8 that, the TG curve of the sample continues to decline from room temperature to 800 °C, and declines rapidly from 250 °C to 450 °C. At 800–900 °C, the TG curve of the sample rise slightly and subsequently remains stable. Meanwhile, it can be seen from the DSC curve that, the curve is basically stable from room temperature to 500 °C. The curve continues to decline in the temperature range of 450∼1000 °C, rise briefly in the temperature range of 1000∼1150 °C, and then continues to decline until to 1200 °C.

Based on the thermogravimetric analysis and the test results above, the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process of silicon nitride-based composites mainly goes through five stages. At the first stage, in the temperature range from room temperature to 100 °C, TG curve declines, indicating that the water in the starting powder evaporates. At the second stage, in the temperature range from 100 °C to 450 °C, TG curve continues to significantly decline, indicating that an obvious weigh loss occurs. Based on the relative references [7,29], it can be concluded that, the phenylpropane side chain of nano-lignin is broken, and a large amount of volatiles such as CO, CH4, CO2 and propionaldehyde are produced in this temperature range. At the third stage, in the temperature range from 450 °C to 800 °C, there is an obvious endothermic stage for the DSC curve, corresponding to the transformation stage from nano-lignin into amorphous carbon. At the fourth stage, in the temperature range of 800∼900 °C, the TG curve gradually rises, and there is a continuous heat absorption stage for DSC curve. Combined with the infrared spectrum analysis, the carbon structure is recombined, the C–N bond is formed, and nitrogen doping occurs. At the fifth stage, at the temperature of 1000 °C or higher, the α-Si3N4 phase in the sample is transformed into the β-Si3N4 phase [31].
Based on all the test and analysis data, the transformation process of Si3N4-based composite in the pyrolysis-hot pressing sintering coupling process can be described as follows.

When lignin is added to the silicon nitride matrix, the lignin carbon structure will change from the benzene ring structure to the carbon six-membered ring structure at the initial stage of sintering (room temperature to 800 °C), forming a solid product (amorphous carbon) [29]. At the same time, gas products (CO, CH4, CO2 and propionaldehyde) are produced by pyrolysis [7,29]. Because the sintering process is in a vacuum state, the gas products will be removed, leaving only solid products. As the temperature further increases to 1700 °C, most of the CC bonds are broken, and finally converted from amorphous carbon to graphene quantum dots after carbon structure reorganization and nitrogen doping reaction. Meanwhile, in the coupling process, the α-Si3N4 phase transforms to the β-Si3N4 phase. Finally, β-Si3N4 columnar grains are covered by an overlayer composed of N-GQDs, and a silicon nitride based composite with a “core-shell” microstructure with excellent mechanical properties is obtained in this study. The coupled process of pyrolytic-hot-pressing sintering for this composite is schematically illustrated in Fig. 9 .

In this study, nano-lignin as a precursor was added into silicon nitride powder, and one novel ceramic composite was obtained by a coupling preparation method of pyrolysis and hot-pressing sintering. During the preparation process, nano-lignin was transformed to N-GQDs, and α-Si3N4 was transformed to β-Si3N4, forming a special “core-shell” microstructure.

During the preparation process, the nano-lignin was transformed to a solid product (amorphous carbon), while the water and gas products (CO, CH4, CO2 and propionaldehyde) evaporated from the sample in the temperature range from room temperature to 800 °C. Subsequently, the amorphous carbon was transformed to N-GQDs, while α-Si3N4 began to transformed to the β-Si3N4 in the temperature range from 800 °C to 1400 °C. Next, all amorphous carbon was transformed to N-GQDs by carbon structure rearrangement and nitrogen doping, while most of the α-Si3N4 was transformed into β-Si3N4 in the temperature range of 1400∼1700 °C. Finally, the β-Si3N4 columnar grains were coated with an overlayer of N-GQDs, resulting in excellent mechanical properties.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors are thankful for the funding provided by the Open Fund of the National Joint Engineering Research Center for abrasion control and molding of metal materials (grant number No. HKDNM201902), Chinese Postdoctoral Science Foundation (grant number No. 2019M653630), Natural Science Foundation of Shaanxi Province (grant number No. 2018JM5056) and Xi'an Science and Technology Program(grant number No.23ZDCYJSGG0047-2022).

Crawford R L. Lignin biodegradation and transformation[M]. Wiley*, 1981.
